explanation.headline: Demystifying the Transformer Neural Network Architecture
explanation.body:
This image illustrates the architecture of a Transformer model, a foundational element in modern AI for tasks like translation and summarization. The model is divided into two main parts—encoders and decoders—each with multiple layers. 

The encoders (on the left) are responsible for processing input data. Each encoder layer contains self-attention mechanisms that allow the network to weigh the relevance of different words in a context, followed by feed-forward neural networks that process these weighted inputs. The 'Add & Normalize' processes after each block ensure stable transformations.

Decoders (on the right) are similar but include an additional step known as 'Encoder-Decoder Attention.' This part links the processed input from encoders to help generate the output. Each decoder layer also includes self-attention and feed-forward networks.

Positional encoding is applied to input sequences to preserve the order of words, which the model doesn't naturally understand. Outputs are finally processed through a linear layer before applying a 'Softmax' to generate probabilities for prediction tasks.
explanation.analogy: Think of encoders and decoders like a wine taster and presenter team. Encoders are the tasters, understanding flavors (input) in depth. Decoders, like the presenters, take these insights to craft a compelling wine story (output), ensuring the audience savors every note.
mechanics: Layers of encoders with self-attention and feed-forward networks.
Decoders apply self-attention and encoder-decoder attention.
Final layers transform outputs into probabilistic predictions with Softmax.
explanation.firstPrinciple: Leverage self-attention to capture contextual dependencies within data sequences.
